Q1: I tried to keep things simple and predictable because my life is already complicated enough. I'd usually set aside time on weekends and maybe two evenings during the week to go through materials. With 16 courses to juggle, I couldn't afford to be disorganized, so I'd make lists of what needed to be done and check things off. It wasn't exciting, but it kept me from falling apart completely.
Q2: I really tried to stick to regular times because that's what works for me. Sundays were always my big study days, and I'd try to do a bit on Tuesday and Thursday evenings. But honestly, when deadlines piled up, everything went out the window and I'd just focus on whatever was due soonest. The routine helped me feel less anxious, even if I couldn't always maintain it.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about a deadline or just habit at this point. I'd log in first thing when I sat down to study, almost automatically. Sometimes I'd check just to see if there was anything new posted or if I'd missed an announcement. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more often because I wanted to see my progress and earn those little badges.
Q4: Yes, after the first few weeks it became automatic. I'd wake up, have my tea, and open the LMS before doing anything else. The gamification elements helped because checking in felt like a small accomplishment even on days when I couldn't do much else. It became like checking my phone—just something I did without thinking about it.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline, honestly. I watched so many hours of them because I learn better when I can see and hear things being explained. The gamified tasks were also really helpful because they broke things into smaller pieces that felt manageable. When you're stressed and low on energy, having bite-sized activities makes a huge difference.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums beyond what was required. I just didn't have the mental energy to engage with other people's opinions when I was struggling to keep up with the basics. Some of the supplementary readings I also skipped because I had to prioritize—with limited resources and time, I focused on what would directly help my grades.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to an assignment or exam, it felt worth doing. The gamified elements helped too because earning points or completing challenges gave me a small sense of accomplishment. I needed activities that gave me something back, even just a feeling of progress, because my motivation was pretty low most of the time.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're managing 16 courses with limited resources and feeling stressed, you have to make hard choices. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones? I'd look at them and think 'maybe later' and later never came. There was always something more urgent.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything so I could study on the bus or when my internet was being unreliable. I'd print materials at the library when I could afford it and study from those. A lot of my actual learning happened with handwritten notes spread out on my bed, working through problems physically. That's how I learn best—doing things with my hands.
Q10: There were many times I'd watch a video once online, download the slides, and then spend hours working through them offline with pen and paper. I'd practice problems repeatedly until my hands remembered the steps. The LMS would just show one video view, but I might have spent three hours afterward actually mastering that content through physical practice.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or video, watch it once to make sure it downloaded correctly, and then study it offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable, and I learned early on to grab everything I needed in one session. So my actual engagement with materials was much deeper than my download count would suggest.
Q12: There were weeks when everything felt impossible. I'd feel this heavy sadness that made even logging in feel like climbing a mountain. Financial stress, feeling isolated because I don't do extracurriculars, and just general exhaustion all piled up. During those times, I'd do the bare minimum to stay afloat and nothing more.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed about money, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities like work and family stuff meant some weeks the LMS got whatever scraps of attention I had left. The confusion from having so many courses also meant I sometimes couldn't focus properly on any single one.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the lecture playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about bills or just feeling sad. I'd scroll through materials, technically 'accessing' them, but not really taking anything in. Those sessions felt hollow, like going through motions without any real learning happening.
Q15: The assignments were the hardest. I'd know they were important but something in me just... couldn't start them. My completion rate was terrible even though I understood the material. It's like there was a wall between knowing I should do something and actually doing it. The sadness made everything feel heavier than it should have been.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my only real motivation. I'd watch the due dates and plan backward from there. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't have done anything optional at all. The gamified tasks helped because they created mini-deadlines that kept me moving even between major assignments. I needed that external pressure.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it lifted my mood and made me want to engage more. The gamification feedback—points, badges, progress bars—actually helped more than I expected. Seeing that I'd accomplished something, even something small, pushed back against the sadness a little. Negative feedback just made me want to withdraw though.
Q18: The structure helped me because I need external organization when my internal motivation is low. Having clear modules and deadlines meant I didn't have to make too many decisions myself. But with 16 courses, the structures sometimes clashed and I'd get overwhelmed trying to keep track of everything. The gamified elements added a structure that felt supportive rather than demanding.
Q19: The gamification made engagement easier because it turned tasks into smaller, achievable goals. That really helped when I was feeling low. What made it harder was when materials were only available online without download options, or when activities required lots of peer interaction. I'm not great at reaching out to others, so those felt like extra barriers.
Q20: I think that's exactly what happens when you're going through the motions but can't actually complete things. You might log in constantly, watch videos, check announcements—all visible activity—but when it comes to actually producing work, something blocks you. For me, it was a combination of low motivation and feeling overwhelmed. The doing felt impossible even when the engaging didn't.
Q21: That makes sense to me because of offline studying. Someone could download everything in one session, barely log in again, but spend hours studying from printed notes or downloaded files. I did reasonably well on exams because I studied hard offline, even though my assignment completion was poor. The LMS only sees part of the picture.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when you log in, how long you spend, what you click on. That's useful for seeing habits. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, whether you're actually learning or just present. They miss offline work, emotional state, and the difference between productive time and just scrolling mindlessly. They're a shadow of the real experience.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with what you're learning. It's when the material gets into your head and you find yourself thinking about it even when you're not studying. For me, engagement happened in quiet moments with my notes, working through problems until they made sense. It's not about time spent online—it's about mental investment.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally while still showing up. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with handwritten notes, physically working through problems because that's how I learn. It wouldn't show that my high video watch time was often the same video replayed because I couldn't focus the first time. And it definitely wouldn't show that the gamified tasks were sometimes the only thing that made me feel like I was accomplishing anything at all.
